title: Jewish paper-cut Mizrach
subtitle: A symmetrical folk paper-cut in the Eastern European Jewish tradition
excerpt: A Mizrach is a decorative east-facing plaque for the home, traditionally made as a symmetrical paper-cut by Jewish communities in Eastern Europe. This tutorial covers the paper-cutting craft: designing a symmetric composition on folded paper, cutting sequence, and mounting on a contrasting backing.
sourceNotes: Folk Mizrachim paper-cuts from Eastern European Jewish communities (approximately 18th to 20th centuries) are in the public domain by age. The symmetric cut-paper tradition these works draw on is shared with the Polish wycinanki and Chinese jianzhi traditions, documented in pre-1928 craft literature. The papercutting method in this tutorial is drawn from general symmetric paper-cutting technique comparable to those described in pre-1928 sources on German scherenschnitte and Chinese jianzhi. No single pre-1928 source specifically documents the Mizrach paper-cut form; this tutorial synthesises the paper-cutting craft from these related traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hebrew for 'east': a decorative wall piece placed in a Jewish home on the east-facing wall to indicate the direction of Jerusalem for prayer.
A paper-cut made by folding the paper in half (or in quarters) before cutting, so that the design is mirrored when the sheet is unfolded.
A cut made within the interior of the design, removing negative space without touching the outer silhouette of the shape. Inside cuts are made before the outer silhouette is cut.

content.text: Folk paper-cuts were made in Jewish communities across Eastern Europe as home decorations, gifts, and everyday objects. The
Mizrach

, a plaque marking the direction of Jerusalem, was one of the most common forms. These paper-cuts combined Hebrew lettering, animal motifs (deer, eagles, lions), and decorative borders. The cut paper was usually white, mounted on a deep blue, red, or black backing. The strong colour contrast makes the work legible from across a room.

This tutorial teaches the paper-cutting craft: folding to plan symmetry, the correct sequence for inside and outside cuts, and mounting on a backing. The Hebrew lettering in the central panel is treated here as a geometric placeholder, since the tutorial teaches cutting not calligraphy. A reader who writes Hebrew can substitute real letterforms in the central panel.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, white or cream
items.qty: One A4 sheet for the main cut
items.substitutions: Cream-toned cartridge gives a warmer result against a deep blue backing. Avoid paper thinner than 160 gsm; it tears at fine cuts.

items.name: Coloured backing paper
items.qty: One A4 sheet, deep blue, red, or black
items.substitutions: Any medium-weight paper (120 gsm or above) in a high-contrast colour. Dark navy gives the most traditional look.

items.name: Scalpel, 10A
items.substitutions: Optional. A scalpel blade is finer than a craft knife and easier to control on tight curves and small interior cuts.

items.name: Light box, A4
items.substitutions: Optional. Useful for tracing the design through the folded paper.

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades; blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler for straight lines, with the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path, never in front of it. Cut away from the body.

content.text: Designing the composition

The classic Mizrach layout has three zones: a central rectangular panel (where the lettering would be), two flanking panels with symmetrical animal or floral motifs, and an outer border frame. All three zones are cut from the single folded sheet; nothing is added.

Fold the A4 cut paper in half vertically, bringing the right edge to meet the left. The fold is the line of symmetry. Draw the design on the right half (the open side) in pencil. Whatever you draw on this half will appear mirrored on the left when the sheet is unfolded. This is the defining logic of
symmetrical cutting
: you only design and cut half the composition.

Plan the design around what will stay (the paper) and what will be removed (the cut-away negative space). Avoid isolated shapes with no connection to the main body; a floating circle will fall out of the design when the surrounding paper is cut away. Every element should connect to at least one other element or to the border.

A simple first layout: a central rectangular space framed by a narrow border (5 mm wide), two flanking panels with a repeated diamond or leaf pattern cut into them, and a wider outer border with a simple notched or scalloped edge. Draw just the right half.
Cutting sequence: inside cuts first
Always make
inside cuts

before the outside silhouette cut. If you cut the outer border first, the remaining piece becomes fragile and difficult to hold steady while working on the interior. Leave the outer border as the last cut of all.

Keeping the paper folded, begin with the smallest interior openings in the flanking panels. Hold the folded stack firmly on the cutting mat. The craft knife passes through both layers at once. For small diamonds and leaf shapes: plunge the knife point at one corner, cut to the opposite corner, rotate 90 degrees, repeat. Lift the waste piece from both layers together.

Work outward from the smallest cuts to the largest. The central panel opening is cut next: four straight cuts, one per side, with a slight overcut at each corner. Then the space around the central panel. Finally, the outer silhouette of the entire Mizrach shape.

Keep the paper flat against the mat throughout. Lifting a partly-cut design to check the progress is tempting but risks tearing a thin bridge between two design elements. Unfolding should wait until all cutting is done.
Unfolding and checking

Once all cuts are made, unfold the paper slowly, pulling the two halves apart along the fold rather than lifting from one corner. Lay the unfolded cut flat on the cutting mat and press gently with a clean hand to flatten the fold crease. Check each cut. Any narrow bridges between design elements that were not quite cut through can be tidied with a single light knife pass; do not pull or tear.
Mounting on backing paper

Lay the backing paper on the cutting mat. Brush a thin, even coat of diluted PVA (1:1 with water) onto the back of the cut paper using the paste brush. Work quickly; the cut paper curls as it absorbs moisture. Set the cut paper glue-side down onto the backing, centred or offset as the composition requires. Press down with a clean hand, working from the centre outward. Lay a sheet of clean paper over the top and press under a flat weight for at least twenty minutes.
When dry, trim the backing paper to match the outer edge of the cut, or leave a border around it as a frame.
Finishing

Press the mounted piece flat under weights overnight before framing or displaying. The PVA continues to tighten as it sets fully. A mounted Mizrach looks its best in a simple card mat inside a glass frame; the glass keeps the fine paper edges from lifting over time.
Care

Paper-cuts are sensitive to humidity. Display away from damp walls and direct sunlight. A framed piece behind UV-filtering glass will hold its colour for many years. Unframed paper-cuts stored flat between sheets of acid-free tissue and kept in a dry drawer remain in good condition.
Variations

A four-fold rather than a two-fold gives a design with four-way symmetry: fold the paper in half vertically, then in half again horizontally. The design drawn in one quarter appears in all four when opened. Four-fold compositions are more constrained; very small negative-space cuts become difficult to execute through four layers of 220 gsm paper; but the visual result can be striking.
The symmetric geometry here is shared with Chinese jianzhi and Polish wycinanki. All three use the fold-and-cut method. The same technique creates very different visual results in each tradition.
